name: Frankie Edgar

qualityWinLedger.context: Won the UFC lightweight title from an elite reigning champion.
Immediate dominant confirmation over the same champion-level opponent; slight repeat discount.
Elite featherweight contender stopped in the first round.
High-end featherweight contender in a title-eliminator-level matchup.
Elite lightweight rival and title challenger.
Highly ranked bantamweight win late in Edgar’s career; close split-decision context noted.
Former UFC lightweight champion and still a strong ranked opponent.
Rising ranked featherweight contender at the time of the win.
Excellent early lightweight win, but before either fighter had established title-level standing.
Elite lighter-weight name, discounted for one-off featherweight timing.
Quality 2013 featherweight win; future championship success is not back-credited.

primeWindowDetail.endReason: Benson, Aldo, and Ortega losses did not end it because Edgar kept re-entering title-level fights. Holloway closes it.

lossContextEvents.context: Occurred before the Sean Sherk prime start.
Immediate title rematch kept Edgar at championship level.
Edgar moved to featherweight and immediately remained title-level.
Edgar rebuilt into another featherweight title fight.
Edgar remained an elite featherweight contender afterward.
Edgar returned to a UFC featherweight title fight against Max Holloway.

shortCase: Edgar is the lightweight champion and three-division longevity case: title wins, elite featherweight wins, legendary durability, and a loss column that keeps him below cleaner champions.
bestArgument: Edgar wins comparisons when title grit, schedule depth, three-division relevance, and elite longevity matter more than clean dominance.
counterArgument: Edgar’s counterargument is simple: his record looks worse than his actual career because he spent years fighting elite and larger opponents across divisions.
whyRankedHere: Edgar ranks here because his UFC-only résumé has real championship value, rare three-division relevance, and a deep quality-win ledger built around B.J. Penn, Gray Maynard, Chad Mendes, Cub Swanson, Charles Oliveira, Urijah Faber, Sean Sherk, Jeremy Stephens, and Tyson Griffin.
whyNotHigher: He does not rank higher because the official loss column is heavy, his title reign was not long enough to match the top champions, and his prime dominance is more about grit, pace, and durability than overwhelming separation.
finalTakeaway: Edgar is a classic UFC-only résumé fighter: not a top-tier GOAT case, but a former lightweight champion with elite longevity, real quality wins, and one of the most respected toughness profiles in UFC history.
